paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked Qibo, saying: 'I once ascended the Terrace of Clear Coolness (Qingleng Tai), and midway up the stairway I turned to look back, then found myself creeping forward on hands and knees, overcome with confusion. I privately found this strange and secretly wondered at it within myself. I closed my eyes and looked again, calmed my heart and settled my qi, but for a long time the confusion did not resolve. My vision reeled and spun; I let my hair hang loose and knelt for a long while, bowing my head to look down, yet even after a long time the disorder did not stop. It came upon me suddenly as I climbed — what qi causes this to happen?' Qibo replied: 'The essence qi of the five viscera and six bowels all ascends to pour into the eyes and becomes their essence. The nest of this essence forms the eye itself; the essence of the bones forms the pupil; the essence of the sinews forms the black of the eye; the essence of the blood forms the network vessels binding the eye's socket; the essence of qi forms the white of the eye; and the essence of the flesh and muscles forms the surrounding enclosure. Together these wrap and gather the essence of sinew, bone, blood, and qi, and join with the vessels to form the eye-system (muxi). Above, this system attaches to the brain; behind, it emerges at the nape of the neck. Hence when evil qi strikes the nape and meets with a deficiency in the body, it penetrates deeply and follows the eye-system inward into the brain. Once it enters the brain, the brain is set spinning; when the brain spins, it pulls the eye-system taut; and when the eye-system is drawn taut, the eyes grow dizzy and everything seems to whirl. When evil strikes the essence itself, and the places struck no longer correspond evenly to one another, the essence scatters; and when the essence scatters, vision divides, so that one sees two objects where there is only one. The eyes are the essence of the five viscera and six bowels, the place where nutritive qi, defensive qi, and the hun and po souls constantly circulate, and where the spirit qi is generated. Hence when the spirit is overtaxed, the hun and po scatter and the will and intent fall into disorder. This is why the pupil and the black of the eye are patterned on yin, while the white of the eye and its red vessels are patterned on yang; only when yin and yang combine and are transmitted together does clear vision result. The eyes are the emissary of the heart, and the heart is the lodging place of the spirit. Hence when the spirit and essence fall into disorder and no longer turn properly, and one suddenly perceives things in unaccustomed places, the essence, spirit, hun, and po scatter and no longer cohere with one another — this is what is called confusion (huo).'

Huangdi said: 'I have suspected this might be so. Whenever I go to the East Garden, I am invariably overcome with confusion, and once I leave it, all returns to normal. Is it that the East Garden alone taxes my spirit? Why should it be so peculiar?' Qibo said: 'It is not so. When the heart delights in something while the spirit finds something distasteful, and the two suddenly clash, the essence qi falls into disorder and vision goes astray — hence confusion arises, and only when the spirit shifts does it return to normal. This is why a mild case is called disorientation (mi), while a severe case is called confusion (huo).'

Huangdi said: 'What qi causes a person to be forgetful?' Qibo said: 'When the qi of the upper body is insufficient while the qi of the lower body has a surplus, the intestines and stomach are replete while the heart and lung are deficient. When they are deficient, the nutritive and defensive qi linger below and, for a long time, fail to ascend at their proper time — hence the person becomes forgetful.'

Huangdi said: 'What qi causes a person to be easily hungry yet have no appetite for food?' Qibo said: 'The essence qi has converged upon the spleen, while heat qi lingers in the stomach. When the stomach is heated it digests grain quickly, and because the grain is digested quickly the person becomes hungry easily. When the stomach qi rebels upward, the stomach duct turns cold, and hence the person has no appetite for food.'

Huangdi said: 'What qi causes a person, when ill, to be unable to sleep?' Qibo said: 'The defensive qi is unable to enter yin and instead lingers constantly in yang. When it lingers in yang, the yang qi becomes full; when the yang qi is full, the Yang Springing Vessel (yangqiao) becomes excessive. Since the defensive qi cannot enter yin, the yin qi becomes deficient — hence the eyes will not close in sleep.'

Huangdi said: 'What qi causes a person's eyes to be afflicted so that they cannot see?' Qibo said: 'The defensive qi lingers in yin and is unable to move into yang. When it lingers in yin, the yin qi becomes exuberant; when the yin qi is exuberant, the Yin Springing Vessel (yinqiao) becomes full. Since the defensive qi cannot enter yang, the yang qi becomes deficient — hence the eyes remain closed.'

Huangdi said: 'What qi causes a person to sleep excessively?' Qibo said: 'In such a person the intestines and stomach are large, the skin is damp, and the interstitial flesh does not separate readily. When the intestines and stomach are large, the defensive qi lingers there for a long time; when the skin is damp and the interstitial flesh fails to separate readily, the qi's movement is slowed. Now, the defensive qi ordinarily travels through yang by day and through yin by night; hence when the yang qi is exhausted one sleeps, and when the yin qi is exhausted one wakes. Thus when the intestines and stomach are large, the defensive qi lingers long as it travels; when the skin is damp and the interstitial flesh fails to separate readily, its movement is slow. Having lingered long in yin, its qi grows turbid rather than clear, so the person longs to close the eyes — hence excessive sleep results. When, by contrast, the intestines and stomach are small, the skin is smooth and relaxed, and the interstitial flesh separates freely, the defensive qi lingers long in yang — hence the person sleeps little.'

Huangdi said: 'What of cases outside this ordinary pattern, where a person suddenly begins to sleep excessively — what qi causes this?' Qibo said: 'Evil qi lingers in the upper burner, and the upper burner becomes closed and impassable. After eating, or after drinking hot broth, the defensive qi lingers long in yin and fails to move — hence the person suddenly sleeps excessively.'

Huangdi said: 'Good. How should one treat all these various evils?' Qibo said: 'First address the viscera and bowels, eliminating their minor faults; afterward regulate the qi, draining what is excessive and supplementing what is deficient. One must first clearly ascertain whether the patient's body and will are at ease or in distress, and only once this is settled should one proceed to needle.'

Huangdi said: 'I have heard that when the intestines and stomach receive grain, the upper burner sends forth qi to warm the interstitial flesh, nourish the bones and joints, and open the interstices of the skin. The middle burner sends forth qi like dew, which ascends to pour into the valleys and ravines of the flesh and seeps into the grandchild vessels (sunmai); there the fluids and humors become harmonized, and are transformed, turning red to become blood. When the blood is harmonious, the grandchild vessels first become full to overflowing, and then pour into the network vessels; when these too are full, they pour into the channel vessels. Once yin and yang have thus become fully extended, the blood moves in step with the breath. Its movement follows a set order, its circuit follows a fixed course, corresponding with Heaven itself, and it never comes to rest. When one palpates the vessels in order to regulate them: proceeding from deficiency, one removes excess — yet if one drains what is already deficient, insufficiency only deepens; if this is done too hastily, the qi is diminished; and if the needle is retained, the effects unfold in their proper sequence, first one thing and then another. Proceeding from excess, one removes deficiency — yet if one supplements what is already in excess, a surplus results. Once the blood and qi have been regulated in this way, the body's form and qi are thereby sustained. I have already learned of the balance and imbalance of blood and qi, but I do not yet know from what welling-abscess (yong) and flat-abscess (ju) arise, the timing of their formation and resolution, nor the term allotted for death or survival, whether near or distant. How is this to be gauged? May I hear of it?'

Qibo said: 'The channel vessels flow without cease, in step with Heaven's own measure and in accord with Earth's own order. Thus when the lodges of Heaven lose their proper measure, the sun and moon become eclipsed and dimmed; when the pathways of Earth lose their proper order, the waterways overflow their banks, the grasses and weeds fail to mature, and the five grains fail to grow; when roads become impassable, the people cease to travel between one another, and those gathered in lanes and settled in towns become cut off and separated from one another. The blood and qi are just the same — allow me to explain the reason. Now the blood vessels, together with the nutritive and defensive qi, circulate ceaselessly, corresponding above to the stars and constellations, and below to the fixed measure of the channels. When cold evil lodges within the channels and network vessels, the blood congeals; when the blood congeals it fails to flow freely; and when it fails to flow freely, the defensive qi converges there and is unable to return along its course — hence welling-abscess and swelling result. The cold qi transforms into heat; when the heat prevails it putrefies the flesh, and when the flesh putrefies it becomes pus. If the pus is not drained away it will rot the sinews; when the sinews rot, the bone is injured; when the bone is injured, the marrow is consumed. If this does not open into a hollow of the bone through which it might drain, and cannot be released and discharged, the blood dries up and the area becomes hollow and empty, so that the sinews, bones, and flesh no longer nourish one another; the channel vessels decay and leak, and the noxious vapor steams upward into the five viscera — once the viscera are injured, death follows.'

Huangdi said: 'I should like to hear in full the various forms that welling-abscess and flat-abscess take, together with the names by which each is known and the dangers to be guarded against.' Qibo said: 'When an abscess breaks out within the throat, it is called mengju, the fierce ju. If fierce ju is not treated, it turns to pus; if the pus is not drained it will block the throat, and the patient dies within half a day. Once it has turned to pus, drain it, apply pig's fat, and have the patient eat cold food; within three days it will be resolved.'

When it breaks out on the neck, it is called yaoju, the fatal ju. The swelling is large and reddish-black; if not treated promptly, the heat qi will descend to Yuanye, the hollow of the armpit, injuring the Conception Vessel in front and steaming the liver and lung within. Once it steams the liver and lung, the patient dies within ten-odd days.

When yang heat lingers and erupts greatly, consuming the brain and lodging in the nape, it is called naoshuo, brain-scorching. Its color is dull and lusterless, and the nape aches as though pierced by a needle. If the patient becomes vexed at heart, death follows and the condition cannot be treated.

When it breaks out on the shoulder or upper arm, it is called ciyong, the blemish welling-abscess. Its appearance is reddish-black; treat it promptly. This will cause the patient to sweat down to the very feet, but it does not harm the five viscera. Once the abscess has been present for four or five days, proceed to cauterize it.

When it breaks out below the armpit, red and hard, it is called miju, the rice-grain ju. Treat it with the stone needle (bianshi); the stone should be fine and long, and the puncturing sparse. Apply pig's fat, and within six days it will heal; do not bind or wrap it. If the swelling is hard and does not rupture into pus, this is what is called saber-scrofula with goiter (madao xieying), a hard chain of swellings like beads along a saber hilt, accompanied by goiter, and it must be treated promptly.

When it breaks out on the chest, it is called jingju, the well ju. Its shape resembles a large bean, and it rises up over three or four days. If not treated early it will sink down into the abdomen; left untreated, the patient dies within seven days.

When it breaks out on the front of the chest, over the breastbone, it is called ganju, the sweet ju. Its color is greenish, and its shape resembles the fruit of a gourd. The patient constantly suffers from chills and fever; treat it promptly to eliminate the chills and fever. Left untreated, the patient dies within ten years, and pus emerges only after death.

When it breaks out on the rib-side, it is called baici, the rotting blemish. The rotting blemish is a disorder that afflicts women; moxibust it. As the disorder progresses it becomes a large welling-abscess full of pus; in treating it, once granulation flesh the size of a small red bean appears within, take one sheng each of zuoling root and qiaocao root, boil them in one dou six sheng of water, and reduce this down to three sheng. Have the patient drink it forcibly, then dress warmly and sit atop the cauldron to receive its warmth, so as to induce sweating down to the very feet; once this is achieved, the patient will recover.

When it breaks out on the thigh or shin, it is called gujingju, the thigh-shin ju. Its outward appearance shows little change, yet the abscess and its pus press against the bone. If not treated promptly, the patient dies within thirty days.
When it breaks out on the buttocks, it is called ruiju, the sharp ju. Its appearance is red, hard, and large; treat it promptly. Left untreated, the patient dies within thirty days.

When it breaks out on the inner thigh, it is called chishi, red-spreading. If not treated promptly, the patient dies within sixty days. Should it appear between the two thighs and go untreated, death should occur within ten days.

When it breaks out on the knee, it is called ciyong, the blemish welling-abscess. Its form is large, but the color of the swelling does not change; the patient suffers chills and fever, and the mass is hard as stone. Do not lance it with the stone needle — one who lances it while it is still hard will die. Only once it has softened should it be lanced; then the patient will live.

In all cases where welling-abscess or flat-abscess breaks out at a joint and its effects correspond straight through to the far side, it cannot be treated. If it breaks out on a yang aspect of the body, the patient dies within a hundred days; if on a yin aspect, within thirty days.
When it breaks out on the shin, it is called tunie, rabbit-bite. Its appearance is red down to the bone; treat it promptly. Left untreated, it will endanger the patient's life.

When it breaks out on the inner ankle, it is called zouhuan, sluggish walking. Its appearance is that of a welling-abscess, and its color does not change. Repeatedly lance its transport point with the stone needle to halt the chills and fever, and the patient will not die.
When it breaks out on the upper or lower foot, it is called siyin, the four excesses. Its form is a large welling-abscess; treat it promptly, for if untreated the patient dies within a hundred days.

When it breaks out at the side of the foot, it is called liyong, the dread welling-abscess. Its form is not large, at first no bigger than the little finger when it erupts; treat it promptly, removing whatever has turned black. If it fails to resolve and instead keeps enlarging, and is left untreated, the patient dies within a hundred days.

When it breaks out on the toe, it is called tuoyong, the sloughing welling-abscess. If its appearance is reddish-black it is fatal and cannot be treated; if it is not reddish-black, it is not fatal. If it fails to subside, amputate it promptly — otherwise the patient will die.

Huangdi said: 'Master, you have spoken of welling-abscess and flat-abscess — how are they to be distinguished from one another?' Qibo said: 'When the nutritive and defensive qi become detained within the channel vessels, the blood congeals and fails to move; when it fails to move, the defensive qi that follows it likewise becomes obstructed and impassable, and being blocked and dammed up, cannot flow — hence heat arises. When the great heat does not cease and the heat prevails, the flesh putrefies, and when the flesh putrefies it becomes pus. Yet in this case the disorder cannot sink in deeply: the bone and marrow are not scorched and withered, and the five viscera are not injured — hence this is termed a welling-abscess (yong).'

Huangdi said: 'What is meant by a flat-abscess (ju)?' Qibo said: 'When the heat qi is thick and exuberant, it sinks down deeply into the flesh and skin; the sinews and marrow wither, and the disorder connects inwardly to the five viscera, exhausting the blood and qi. Beneath the site of the swelling, the sinews, bones, and healthy flesh are all consumed without remainder — hence this is termed a flat-abscess (ju). In a flat-abscess, the skin above is lusterless and hard, thick like the skin of an ox's neck. In a welling-abscess, the skin above is thin and glossy. These are the signs by which the two are distinguished.'
